fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a structured logging serializer library that our downstream pipeline can actually consume without us maintaining a bunch of fragile regex rules. Right now every service is hand-rolling their own JSON and the dashboards keep breaking because field names are inconsistent or timestamps are in different formats. We talked about this in the last infra sync — basically the same approach we used for that ECS alignment work, just applied here as a reusable library.

The library needs to take in log event data (time, severity, message, some optional context stuff) and spit out one clean JSON line per event. Timestamps should always look the same. Severity labels should line up visually in the output. We also need to support attaching key/value context to events — most keys should be namespaced to avoid collisions, but some well-known correlation keys (like trace and transaction identifiers) should be promoted to top-level. Tags should be supported too as an array. Errors need their own structured fields.

Also really important: the thing needs to handle bad inputs gracefully — no raw stack traces or internal error objects leaking into stdout. There should be a clean, consistent error reporting format that downstream tooling can parse. The adapter layer that reads commands and writes output must be separate from the actual serialization logic. Ping me if unclear on what 'consistent error format' means here, I can dig up the notes.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: build_log_line(timestamp_millis, level, message, service_name=None, thread_name=None, logger_name=None, tags=None, labels=None, top_level_labels=None, error=None) -> str

trigger_keywords: how should the time field look
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The timestamp must always be formatted as a fixed-width UTC ISO-8601 string in the exact form YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ss.SSSZ. Every component is zero-padded to a constant width: 4-digit year, 2-digit month, 2-digit day, 2-digit hour, 2-digit minute, 2-digit second, 3-digit millisecond. The timezone is always the literal character Z (never an offset). For example, epoch 0 yields 1970-01-01T00:00:00.000Z.

trigger_keywords: how are short level names padded
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The log.level value must be right-aligned to a width of five characters by left-padding short level names with spaces inside the quoted region. A level name shorter than five characters gets leading spaces before the opening quote of its value (e.g., WARN becomes ' WARN' and INFO becomes ' INFO' with one leading space each), while a level name of five or more characters (like DEBUG, ERROR, TRACE) gets no padding. This space is literal, inside the JSON string value.

The first three fields in the output JSON line must always appear in this fixed order with this fixed spacing: @timestamp, then log.level, then message. After those three, when present and in this order, come: tags (if any), service.name, process.thread.name, log.logger, then labels fields, then error fields. The spacing after the first three field separators uses a single space after the colon, while subsequent optional fields do not include that extra space.

By default, every label key in the 'labels' input map is prefixed with 'labels.' in the output JSON (e.g., key 'foo' becomes the field 'labels.foo'). This prevents user-supplied context from colliding with reserved top-level ECS fields. When the labels object is absent or empty, no label fields are emitted at all.

A configurable allow-list of key names causes certain labels to be emitted as bare top-level fields without the 'labels.' prefix. The default allow-list covers correlation identifiers such as trace.id, transaction.id, span.id, error.id, and service.name. The caller may override this allow-list by supplying a 'top_level_labels' array in the command. Keys in the allow-list are emitted without prefix; all other keys keep the 'labels.' prefix. Both promoted and namespaced keys can coexist in the same input map and preserve insertion order.

When at least one tag is present, a 'tags' JSON array field is emitted immediately after the 'message' field and before 'service.name'. The tags appear in the order supplied. When the tags array is absent or empty, the 'tags' field is omitted entirely from the output.

An error is serialized into exactly three fields in fixed order: error.code (the error type/class name), error.message (the human-readable message), and error.stack_trace (the rendered stack trace as a single quoted string). Embedded newlines in the stack trace are preserved as the two-character JSON escape sequence \n. When embedded in a build_log_line command, these three error fields are appended after the logger and label fields within the same single JSON line.

trigger_keywords: what happens when input is wrong
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the adapter cannot honor a command, it prints a normalized two-line error block to stdout: first line is 'error=<category>' and second line is '<field>=<value>' with the offending datum. Categories are: 'missing_field' (required field absent, with field=<name>), 'unknown_operation' (op not recognized, with op=<name>), and 'invalid_input' (payload was not a JSON object, with payload=<raw>). No host exception class names, stack traces, runtime messages, or object representations ever appear in stdout.

The codebase must be split into at least two distinct units: a core serialization module (timestamp formatting, string escaping, log-line assembly, error rendering) and a separate execution adapter (reads JSON commands from stdin, calls the core, writes output to stdout). The core must have no knowledge of stdin/stdout or JSON command parsing. The adapter depends on the core, never the reverse. This is not a single-file monolith.

trigger_keywords: how are quotes handled in values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All string values in the output JSON must be correctly escaped according to JSON string rules: a double-quote character becomes \", a backslash becomes \\, and an embedded newline becomes the two-character escape sequence \n. This applies to all string fields including message, label values, error fields, and tag names.

The format_timestamp operation accepts a JSON command object with op='format_timestamp' and an integer field 'epoch_millis'. The output is the formatted timestamp string followed by a single trailing newline character — just the timestamp string, not wrapped in a JSON object. If epoch_millis is missing or not an integer, the normalized error contract applies with field=epoch_millis.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same approach we used for that ECS alignment work
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Elastic Common Schema (ECS) field naming conventions used in the output: @timestamp, log.level, service.name, process.thread.name, log.logger, error.code, error.message, error.stack_trace — as defined in the PRD and test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/

context_pointers.vague_reference: some well-known correlation keys should be promoted to top-level
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The default allow-list of top_level_labels keys: trace.id, transaction.id, span.id, error.id, service.name — as used in feature3_2_labels_top_level.json test cases

context_pointers.vague_reference: clean, consistent error reporting format that downstream tooling can parse
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The normalized two-line error contract: line 1 is 'error=<category>' and line 2 is '<field>=<value>', with categories missing_field, unknown_operation, and invalid_input — as specified in the Architecture & Engineering Constraints section of start.md

context_pointers.vague_reference: severity labels should line up visually in the output
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Right-alignment of log.level values to a width of 5 characters by left-padding with spaces inside the JSON string value (e.g., WARN → ' WARN', INFO → ' INFO'), as demonstrated in feature2_log_line_core.json test cases

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect in my notes right now. Please check the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for concrete input/output examples, and refer to the architectural constraints around separation of the core serializer from the I/O adapter. If it's about field naming or ordering, the ECS alignment work from before should be the reference point.

feature1_iso_timestamp.json.description: Serialize an epoch timestamp given in milliseconds into a fixed-width UTC ISO-8601 date-time string of the form YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ss.SSSZ. Every component is zero-padded to constant width (4-digit year, 2-digit month/day/hour/minute/second, 3-digit millisecond) and the zone is always literal 'Z' (UTC). The input is a JSON object with op 'format_timestamp' and an integer field epoch_millis (milliseconds since the Unix epoch). The output is the formatted string followed by a trailing newline.

feature2_log_line_core.json.description: Assemble a single structured log record into one line of ECS-compatible JSON. The input is a JSON object with op 'build_log_line' and the fields timestamp_millis (epoch ms), level (severity name), message, and optional service_name, thread_name, logger_name. The output is exactly one JSON line terminated by a newline. The first three fields always appear in fixed order and with fixed spacing: @timestamp (formatted as a UTC ISO-8601 string), log.level (right-aligned to a width of five characters by left-padding short level names with spaces, then quoted), and message. After those come, when present, service.name, process.thread.name and log.logger. Fields whose source value is absent are simply omitted.

feature3_1_labels_prefixed.json.description: Attach arbitrary key/value context (a labels map) to a log record. Each label is emitted as its own JSON field after the logger field. By default every label key is namespaced with a 'labels.' prefix so that user context cannot collide with reserved top-level fields. The input provides the labels under the optional 'labels' object; insertion order of the labels is preserved in the output. The output is the full log line with one prefixed field per label.

feature3_2_labels_top_level.json.description: Promote selected context keys to reserved top-level fields instead of namespacing them under 'labels.'. A configurable allow-list of key names (defaulting to correlation identifiers such as trace, transaction, span, error id and service name) is emitted without the 'labels.' prefix, while any other key keeps the prefix. The input supplies the labels under 'labels' and may override the allow-list via the optional 'top_level_labels' array. The output shows the promoted key as a bare top-level field.

feature4_tags.json.description: Render a hierarchy of named markers/tags as a JSON array field named 'tags'. The input supplies an ordered list of tag names under the optional 'tags' array (a flattened parent/child/grandchild marker tree). When at least one tag is present, the record gains a 'tags' field holding the names in order, placed after the message and before the service field. When no tags are supplied the field is omitted entirely.

feature5_1_error_fields.json.description: Serialize an error into the three ECS error fields in fixed order: error.code (the error type name), error.message (the human-readable message), and error.stack_trace (the rendered stack trace as a single quoted string with embedded newlines preserved as the escape sequence backslash-n). The input is a JSON object with op 'format_error' and an 'error' object carrying type, message and stack_trace. The type, message and stack trace are treated as opaque domain-supplied text. The output is a one-line JSON object holding exactly these three fields, terminated by a newline.

feature5_2_error_in_log_line.json.description: Emit a complete ERROR-level log line that carries an attached error. The error.code, error.message and error.stack_trace fields are appended after the logger and label fields, within the same single JSON line. The input is a 'build_log_line' command whose optional 'error' object supplies the error type, message and stack_trace. The output is one JSON line: the standard leading fields followed by the three error fields, terminated by a newline.
